properties.name: Al Baydha Project
properties.summary: Desert regreening at Al Baydha, Saudi Arabia — a self-reinforcing savannah revived without irrigation.
highlights: 50 km south of Mecca in the rocky Hijaz foothills, Saudi Arabia
2,400 hectares of overgrazed desert revived into a working savannah
Founded in 2009 by Neal Spackman, Princess Haifa Al-Faisal, and Mona Hamdy
Rock terraces, gabion check dams, and swales catch and hold rare rainfall
Native and drought-tolerant trees establish without irrigation
Canopy begets cooler soil, more infiltration, more seed bank, more canopy — greening continues on its own

images.alt: Bedouin with camels on the sparse acacia bushland of the Hijaz foothills
Al Baydha rocky foothills before terracing — dry, bare slopes
Rock terraces and gabion check dams laid into the hillside to catch runoff
Community stonemasons building the terrace walls by hand
First seedlings planted into the newly terraced slopes
Moringa and native species taking hold — the first generation of canopy
Al Baydha terraces with established vegetation — years after rainwater harvesting
A working savannah at sunset — 2,400 hectares regreened, continuing without irrigation

properties.history_note: Al Baydha ground work began in 2009.

properties.name: Knepp Estate Rewilding
properties.summary: Large-scale rewilding on former farmland in West Sussex, where free-roaming grazing animals, scrub succession, and dynamic habitat mosaics drive biodiversity recovery. Isabella Tree's book Wilding helped make Knepp one of the defining public stories of modern rewilding — and its water story is as important as its wildlife one: a de-canalized, floodplain-reconnected River Adur and an enclosed beaver reintroduction now feeding England's push toward licensed wild beaver releases.
highlights: Environment Agency removed the river's largest weir in 2012; sea trout were migrating upstream again within about a year
From 2016, artificial banks along the River Adur were removed, letting the river reflood its natural meadows instead of staying channelized
The River Adur restoration won the UK River Prize's Innovative & Novel Project award in 2015
Beavers reintroduced into a river-corridor enclosure in February 2020 — absent from Sussex for centuries — have since bred twice, producing four kits
A 2025 UK government policy change opens the door for Knepp's beaver offspring to be released into open river catchments rather than staying enclosed

images.alt: Free-ranging longhorn cattle among scrub and mature oaks at Knepp
Tamworth sow rooting in the rewilded landscape at Knepp
Returning white storks nesting at Knepp after a 600-year absence in Britain
Fallow deer grazing in the dynamic habitat mosaic of Knepp

properties.history_note: Knepp rewilding pivot began in 2001.

properties.name: Apricot Lane Farms
properties.summary: Regenerative mixed farm system documented in a feature film.
properties.history_note: Apricot Lane Farms regenerative transition began in 2011.

properties.name: Loess Plateau Restoration
properties.summary: Landscape restoration and watershed recovery across degraded terrain.
properties.history_note: The Loess Plateau Watershed Rehabilitation Project launched in 1999, with prior small-scale terracing dating to the 1970s-80s. MODIS starts Feb 2000, so pre-intervention baseline is unobservable from space — EarthPulse shows the post-intervention trajectory only.

properties.name: White Oak Pastures
properties.summary: Regenerative grazing, diversified livestock, rural community revival.
properties.history_note: White Oak Pastures began its regenerative transition under Will Harris in the mid-1990s. MODIS starts Feb 2000, so EarthPulse shows only the post-transition trajectory.

properties.name: Africa Centre for Holistic Management
properties.summary: Allan Savory's flagship landscape restoration site using holistic planned grazing.

images.alt: Dimbangombe Ranch — the 3,200-hectare Africa Centre for Holistic Management in Zimbabwe
Planned-grazing cattle herd at Dimbangombe restoring savanna grassland
Dimbangombe, 2004 — degraded baseline before holistic management
Dimbangombe, 2007 — same site three years later under holistic planned grazing

properties.history_note: Africa Centre for Holistic Management founded at Dimbangombe in the early 2000s.

properties.name: India Water Cup (Paani Foundation)
properties.summary: Community-led watershed restoration movement across Maharashtra, India, where villages train in decentralized watershed management and then compete in a 45-day Water Cup to build soil and water conservation structures at massive scale.

images.alt: Water harvesting structures built through the Paani Foundation Water Cup in Maharashtra
Navnath Bhosle of Velu village, featured in Paani Foundation Water Cup stories of change
Community labor and watershed transformation work in Maharashtra during the Water Cup

links.text: Wilding the Watershed with Villagers: India Water Revolution #1

properties.history_note: Paani Foundation Water Cup launched in 2016.

properties.name: UN World Food Programme Sahel Half-Moon Regreening
properties.summary: Large-scale land restoration in the Sahel using half-moon water-harvesting earthworks to capture runoff, restore vegetation, improve fodder and food security, and support aquifer recharge.
properties.size: Hundreds of thousands of hectares across the Sahel

links.text: WFP: World Water Day - half-moons and aquifer recharge
FAO: How the UN is Holding Back the Sahara Desert
FAO: Inside Africa's Food Forest Mega-Project

videos.title: How the UN is Holding Back the Sahara Desert

properties.history_note: WFP Integrated Resilience programme in the Sahel scaled from 2014.

properties.name: Bridge Creek Beaver Dam Analog Restoration
properties.summary: A degraded, incised tributary of Oregon's John Day River rehydrated with 120+ beaver dam analogs — simple wooden-post-and-willow structures that let real beavers take over, raising the water table and reconnecting the stream to its floodplain along a 32 km reach. NASA has used Landsat imagery to track the resulting vegetation and wetland expansion.

images.alt: Beaver dam analog structures built to encourage beaver activity and restore stream habitat

links.text: NOAA Fisheries: Oregon Beavers Engineer Better Fish Habitat

properties.history_note: Bridge Creek became an Intensively Monitored Watershed in 2007; BDA installation began in 2009.

properties.name: Tarun Bharat Sangh Johad Network
properties.summary: Rajendra Singh (the 'Waterman of India') and Tarun Bharat Sangh organized villages across Rajasthan's Alwar district to rebuild traditional johads — small earthen check dams — at enormous scale. Over 11,800 structures across 1,200+ villages raised groundwater levels an average of 6 meters and brought five rivers, including the Arvari, back to perennial flow after decades dry.

images.alt: Portrait of Dr. Rajendra Singh, the "Waterman of India"
A johad (rainwater-harvesting check dam) in rural Rajasthan

links.text: CEEW: Johad Water Conservation Case Study

properties.history_note: Tarun Bharat Sangh's johad-building began in the mid-1980s and the Arvari river returned to perennial flow by 1995 — well before the MODIS satellite record starts (Feb 2000), so EarthPulse can only show the post-intervention trajectory.

properties.name: Mulloon Rehydration Initiative
properties.summary: A landscape-scale natural sequence farming project on Mulloon Creek in New South Wales, Australia, using willow-anchored 'leaky weirs' to slow, spread, and re-infiltrate flow. What began as a 3 km pilot in 2006 has grown to stream rehabilitation across 16 properties spanning roughly 23,000 hectares, with peer-reviewed monitoring showing sustained dry-season flow and major gains in soil carbon and nutrients.

images.alt: Leaky weir at Mulloon Creek
Westview Farm, Autumn 2020, after rehydration works

links.text: The Conversation: Why Is Everyone Talking About Natural Sequence Farming?

properties.history_note: The Mulloon Creek leaky-weir pilot began in 2006, later scaling into the multi-property Mulloon Rehydration Initiative.

properties.name: Carzalia Valley Produce
properties.summary: Commercial-scale Pima cotton, onion, and chile operation in New Mexico using Advancing Eco Agriculture's plant-sap-analysis and biological-input program. James Johnson reported 1,490 lbs/acre cotton yield in 2020 against an 880 lbs/acre county average.

images.alt: James Johnson with sons and father at their Columbus, NM operation

properties.history_note: Exact date of regenerative transition not publicly documented; 2020 yield figure is the earliest confirmed data point from Advancing Eco Agriculture's case-study coverage.

properties.name: Omeg Family Orchards
properties.summary: Commercial cherry orchards in the Columbia River Gorge running Advancing Eco Agriculture's biological nutrition program. Mike Omeg's operation reported an 89% increase in trunk cross-sectional area versus a control group — the most formally-structured trial in AEA's public case-study set.

images.alt: Cherry trees in full spring blossom

properties.history_note: Exact date of regenerative transition not publicly documented.

properties.name: Keith Morter Farm
properties.summary: Second-generation dryland wheat operation in Gilliam County, Oregon, that reduced nitrogen use and adopted holistic biological management, reporting qualitative gains in soil health, crop resilience, and pest resistance.
properties.history_note: Exact date of regenerative transition not publicly documented; no yield or Brix figures were published alongside the qualitative claims.

properties.name: Jake Ewing Farm
properties.summary: A 1,300-acre corn operation in western Illinois that added foliar biological nutrition and cut herbicide/fungicide use, reporting yields of 275-285 bu/acre against a backdrop of large-scale commodity competition.
properties.history_note: Only a general 'western Illinois' location is publicly documented, not a specific town — this pin is an approximate regional placement, not the exact farm site.

properties.name: Swift River Pecans
properties.summary: First-generation pecan grower Troy Swift's central Texas orchards, where a biological nutrition program is credited with up to 6x yield increases in some varieties while cutting nitrogen inputs from 80-100 lbs/acre to 2.4 lbs/acre and eliminating herbicides.

images.alt: Swift River Pecans building exterior at sunset

properties.history_note: Troy Swift began farming at age 42; exact start year of the regenerative program not confirmed from public sources.

properties.name: Alexandre Family Farm
properties.summary: Organic A2/A2 dairy on the Northern California coast — Advancing Eco Agriculture's first 'Groundwork' consulting client as of April 2025. No quantified results have been published yet.

images.alt: Alexandre Family Farm homepage banner

properties.history_note: AEA's Groundwork consulting relationship began April 2025; too recent for any outcome data yet.

properties.name: Singing Frogs Farm
properties.summary: Elizabeth and Paul Kaiser's 8-acre no-till vegetable farm in Sebastopol, California — the best independently-verified case study in EarthPulse's regenerative-agriculture research. A study published in the 2022 book 'What Your Food Ate' measured water infiltration ~7x greater, soil carbon >400% higher, and soil biology ~300% higher than a neighboring tilled organic farm; a separate 10-year UC Davis study measured soil organic matter reaching 1.2%.

images.alt: Elizabeth and Paul Kaiser of Singing Frogs Farm

properties.history_note: Farming since 2007; independent measurements are from a 10-year UC Davis study and a study published in the 2022 book 'What Your Food Ate.'

properties.name: Paicines Ranch — Vineyard Grazing
properties.summary: Vineyard Director Kelly Mulville's sheep-integrated vineyard in San Benito County, California: an unusually tall overhead trellis keeps fruit above grazing height, letting 1,700+ sheep graze the whole vineyard in a single day year-round, eliminating tillage, mowing, herbicide, and hand-weeding. Ranch-reported gains include soil organic matter up 66% (2014-2021) and insect abundance 5x a conventional neighboring vineyard.

images.alt: Sheep grazing under the tall trellis vineyard system
Portrait of Kelly Mulville, vineyard/grazing director

properties.history_note: Vineyard planted in two phases (12.5 acres in 2017, 12 more in 2020); first in-season grazing summer 2021; first harvest September 2021.

properties.name: Squeeze Citrus
properties.summary: Herb Young left a 36-year career in pesticide research and development at Bayer to build a regenerative citrus grove near Thomasville, Georgia. His own same-day-harvest comparison against two neighboring conventional groves, tested at a USDA-certified lab, found vitamin, flavonoid, and carotenoid levels 8x higher on average, with three flavonoids entirely absent from the conventional fruit.

images.alt: Squeeze Citrus grove

properties.history_note: Herb Young retired after 36-40 years at Bayer and spent about a year studying regenerative agriculture before planting; exact planting year is approximate based on available interviews.

properties.name: First Light Farms
properties.summary: A network of nearly 200 farmers across New Zealand's North and South Islands, founded in 2006 to supply 100% grass-fed wagyu beef and venison outside the commodity meat system. Individual member farms — like the Griffin family's 4th-generation property at Rangiuru, diversified across bush, forestry, avocados, kiwifruit, and pasture — are moving away from soluble fertilizer and agrichemicals toward regenerative management.

images.alt: Pastoral landscape at dusk

links.text: Force of Nature Meats: Supplier Highlight

properties.history_note: First Light was founded in 2006; the regenerative-transition timeline for individual member farms (e.g. the Griffin family at Rangiuru) is not separately documented, so this entry represents the network's founding, not a specific farm's conversion date.
